BC students apply this in more complex scenarios and connect to polar area (a different formula) and parametric applications. AP exam note: area between curves is commonly tested. Students must identify upper and lower functions and find correct intersection points. Vocabulary: area between curves, upper function, lower function, intersection, limits of integration, top minus bottom.

Applications of Integration.skill: CHA-5.A Applications of Integration - Change: Finding the Area Between Curves Expressed as Functions of y
Applications of Integration.prompt: This topic covers area between curves as functions of y for AP Calculus BC (CED: CHA-5.A, Unit 8). A = integral from c to d of [f(y)-g(y)]dy where f(y) is rightmost, g(y) is leftmost. Use horizontal slicing when integrating with respect to y is simpler. Limits c, d are y-values at intersections. BC students should choose between dx and dy integration based on problem structure. AP exam note: students must be able to determine whether integrating with respect to x or y is more efficient. Vocabulary: functions of y, horizontal slicing, right minus left, intersection, integrate with respect to y.

Applications of Integration.skill: CHA-5.A Applications of Integration - Change: Finding the Area Between Curves That Intersect at More Than Two Points
Applications of Integration.prompt: This topic covers area between curves with multiple intersections for AP Calculus BC (CED: CHA-5.A, Unit 8). Find all intersection points; determine which function is on top in each sub-interval; integrate |f(x)-g(x)| by splitting at crossing points; sum the sub-areas. BC students should handle more complex curve configurations and may encounter this with polar curves as well. AP exam note: students must find all intersection points and handle each region correctly. Careful setup prevents sign errors. Vocabulary: multiple intersections, sub-regions, crossing points, piecewise integration, total area.

Applications of Integration.skill: CHA-5.B Applications of Integration - Change: Volumes with Cross Sections: Squares and Rectangles
Applications of Integration.prompt: This topic covers volumes with square and rectangular cross sections for AP Calculus BC (CED: CHA-5.B, Unit 8). V = integral of A(x)dx where A(x) is the cross-section area. For squares with side s(x): A = s^2. For rectangles: A = s*h where dimensions depend on the base region. s(x) is typically the distance between curves. Students visualize 3D solids by stacking cross sections. AP exam note: cross-section volume is a common free-response question. Students must express A(x) clearly and set up the integral correctly. Vocabulary: cross section, perpendicular, base region, square, rectangle, side length, volume integral.

Applications of Integration.skill: CHA-5.B Applications of Integration - Change: Volumes with Cross Sections: Triangles and Semicircles
Applications of Integration.prompt: This topic covers volumes with triangular and semicircular cross sections for AP Calculus BC (CED: CHA-5.B, Unit 8). For equilateral triangles with side s: A = (sqrt(3)/4)s^2. For isosceles right triangles with leg s: A = s^2/2. For semicircles with diameter s: A = (pi/8)s^2. The base region determines s(x). V = integral of A(x)dx. AP exam note: students must know the area formulas for each cross-section type. Careful determination of s(x) is critical. Vocabulary: equilateral triangle, isosceles right triangle, semicircle, diameter, area formula, cross section, volume.

Applications of Integration.skill: CHA-5.C Applications of Integration - Change: Volume with Disc Method: Revolving Around the x- or y-Axis
Applications of Integration.prompt: This topic covers the disc method for AP Calculus BC (CED: CHA-5.C, Unit 8). For revolution around x-axis: V = pi * integral of [f(x)]^2 dx. For revolution around y-axis: V = pi * integral of [g(y)]^2 dy. Each cross section is a disc with radius = distance from curve to axis. AP exam note: disc method problems are common. The most frequent error is forgetting to square the function or forgetting pi. Students should sketch the region to correctly identify the radius. Vocabulary: disc method, solid of revolution, axis of rotation, radius, cross-sectional area, revolve.

Applications of Integration.skill: CHA-5.C Applications of Integration - Change: Volume with Disc Method: Revolving Around Other Axes
Applications of Integration.prompt: This topic covers the disc method for non-standard axes in AP Calculus BC (CED: CHA-5.C, Unit 8). For revolution around y = k or x = k, the radius = distance from curve to axis = |f(x)-k| or |g(y)-k|. V = pi * integral of [radius]^2 dx (or dy). Students must determine the correct distance formula based on the geometry. AP exam note: revolution around non-standard axes is frequently tested. Draw diagrams showing the axis and identify the radius correctly. Vocabulary: disc method, non-standard axis, distance, radius, revolution, horizontal line, vertical line.

Applications of Integration.skill: CHA-5.C Applications of Integration - Change: Volume with Washer Method: Revolving Around the x- or y-Axis
Applications of Integration.prompt: This topic covers the washer method for AP Calculus BC (CED: CHA-5.C, Unit 8). V = pi * integral of [R^2 - r^2]dx where R = outer radius (farther curve to axis), r = inner radius (closer curve). The washer = disc with hole. Do NOT compute pi*(R-r)^2; must subtract squares. For revolution around y-axis, integrate with respect to y. AP exam note: the (R^2 - r^2) vs (R-r)^2 distinction is critical. Students should clearly label R and r. Vocabulary: washer method, outer radius, inner radius, annulus, subtract squares, solid of revolution.

Applications of Integration.skill: CHA-5.C Applications of Integration - Change: Volume with Washer Method: Revolving Around Other Axes
Applications of Integration.prompt: This topic covers washer method for non-standard axes in AP Calculus BC (CED: CHA-5.C, Unit 8). For revolution around y = k: R(x) = |farther curve - k|, r(x) = |closer curve - k|. V = pi * integral of [R^2 - r^2]dx. Drawing a representative rectangle perpendicular to the axis and rotating it helps identify radii. Students must carefully determine outer vs. inner based on distance from the axis. AP exam note: this combines non-standard axis and washer challenges. Meticulous setup with labeled radii is essential. Vocabulary: washer method, non-standard axis, outer radius, inner radius, distance, representative rectangle.

Applications of Integration.skill: CHA-6.A Applications of Integration - Change: The Arc Length of a Smooth, Planar Curve and Distance Traveled (BC ONLY)
Applications of Integration.prompt: This topic covers arc length for AP Calculus BC (CED: CHA-6.A, Unit 8). For y = f(x): L = integral of sqrt(1+[f'(x)]^2)dx from a to b. For parametric x = f(t), y = g(t): L = integral of sqrt([f'(t)]^2+[g'(t)]^2)dt from a to b. The parametric integrand is the speed, so arc length = integral of speed. This is BC ONLY. Key concepts: requires a smooth curve (continuous derivative); most arc length integrals need a calculator. Distance traveled by a particle equals the arc length of its trajectory. AP exam note: arc length setup is commonly tested. Students should set up the integral correctly even if calculator evaluation is needed. Vocabulary: arc length, smooth curve, speed, distance traveled, parametric, Pythagorean, BC only.

Parametric Equations, Polar Coordinates,.skill: CHA-3.G, FUN-8.A Parametric Equations, Polar Coordinates, and Vector-Valued Functions - Change: Defining and Differentiating Parametric Equations
Parametric Equations, Polar Coordinates,.prompt: This topic covers parametric differentiation for AP Calculus BC (CED: CHA-3.G, FUN-8.A, Unit 9). Parametric curves x = f(t), y = g(t) have dy/dx = (dy/dt)/(dx/dt), provided dx/dt != 0. Horizontal tangents: dy/dt = 0, dx/dt != 0. Vertical tangents: dx/dt = 0, dy/dt != 0. The parameter t determines direction of travel. Students find tangent line equations at specific t-values. BC students use parametric differentiation extensively in motion problems and curve analysis. AP exam note: parametric differentiation is a core BC topic. Students must clearly apply the Chain Rule and handle horizontal/vertical tangent cases. Vocabulary: parametric equations, parameter, dy/dx, Chain Rule, horizontal tangent, vertical tangent, direction.

Parametric Equations, Polar Coordinates,.skill: FUN-8.B Parametric Equations, Polar Coordinates, and Vector-Valued Functions - Change: Second Derivatives of Parametric Equations
Parametric Equations, Polar Coordinates,.prompt: This topic covers parametric second derivatives for AP Calculus BC (CED: FUN-8.B, Unit 9). d^2y/dx^2 = (d/dt[dy/dx])/(dx/dt). Steps: compute dy/dx = (dy/dt)/(dx/dt), differentiate with respect to t using the Quotient Rule, then divide by dx/dt. A common error is computing d^2y/dt^2 / d^2x/dt^2 (WRONG). The second derivative determines concavity of the parametric curve. AP exam note: the parametric second derivative formula is frequently tested and commonly misapplied. Students must use the correct formula and show all steps. Vocabulary: second derivative, parametric, concavity, Quotient Rule, d/dt, correct formula.

Parametric Equations, Polar Coordinates,.skill: CHA-6.A Parametric Equations, Polar Coordinates, and Vector-Valued Functions - Change: Finding Arc Lengths of Curves Given by Parametric Equations
Parametric Equations, Polar Coordinates,.prompt: This topic covers parametric arc length for AP Calculus BC (CED: CHA-6.A, Unit 9). L = integral from a to b of sqrt([dx/dt]^2+[dy/dt]^2)dt. The integrand is the speed. Arc length = integral of speed over time. The curve must be smooth. Students set up and evaluate (often with calculator). Example: unit circle x = cos t, y = sin t, t in [0,2pi]: L = 2pi. AP exam note: setting up parametric arc length integrals is commonly tested. Vocabulary: arc length, parametric, speed, smooth curve, integral, Pythagorean theorem.

Parametric Equations, Polar Coordinates,.skill: FUN-8.A, CHA-3.G Parametric Equations, Polar Coordinates, and Vector-Valued Functions - Change: Defining and Differentiating Vector-Valued Functions
Parametric Equations, Polar Coordinates,.prompt: This topic covers vector-valued functions for AP Calculus BC (CED: FUN-8.A, CHA-3.G, Unit 9). r(t) = <x(t), y(t)> gives position. v(t) = r'(t) = <x'(t), y'(t)> is velocity. a(t) = v'(t) = <x''(t), y''(t)> is acceleration. Speed = |v(t)| = sqrt([x']^2+[y']^2). The velocity vector is tangent to the path. At rest when v = <0,0>. BC students analyze planar motion using vectors, distinguishing velocity (vector) from speed (scalar). AP exam note: vector-valued function problems are a signature BC topic. Students must compute velocity, acceleration, and speed accurately. Vocabulary: vector-valued function, position, velocity, acceleration, speed, magnitude, component, tangent.

Parametric Equations, Polar Coordinates,.skill: FUN-8.C Parametric Equations, Polar Coordinates, and Vector-Valued Functions - Analysis of Functions: Integrating Vector-Valued Functions
Parametric Equations, Polar Coordinates,.prompt: This topic covers integrating vector-valued functions for AP Calculus BC (CED: FUN-8.C, Unit 9). Integrate component-wise: integral of <f(t), g(t)>dt = <integral f dt, integral g dt>. Position from velocity: r(t) = r(t0) + integral from t0 to t of v(s)ds. Velocity from acceleration: v(t) = v(t0) + integral from t0 to t of a(s)ds. Displacement = integral of v dt (vector). Total distance = integral of |v| dt = integral of speed (scalar, not the same as displacement magnitude). AP exam note: students must distinguish displacement (vector) from total distance (scalar). Initial conditions determine particular solutions component-wise. Vocabulary: component-wise integration, displacement, total distance, initial condition, vector integral.

Parametric Equations, Polar Coordinates,.skill: CHA-3.G, FUN-8.A Parametric Equations, Polar Coordinates, and Vector-Valued Functions - Analysis of Functions: Solving Motion Problems Using Parametric and Vector-Valued Functions
Parametric Equations, Polar Coordinates,.prompt: This topic covers solving planar motion problems for AP Calculus BC (CED: CHA-3.G, FUN-8.A, Unit 9). Students synthesize parametric/vector tools: find position, velocity, acceleration at given times; determine when particle is at rest; find slope of path (dy/dx = y'/x'); compute speed; find total distance; determine position using integrals and initial conditions. BC exam signature problem: given v(t) and initial position, find position at time t by integrating components. AP exam note: motion problems are among the most common BC free-response questions. Students should organize work clearly, label vectors, and distinguish scalar from vector quantities. Vocabulary: motion, parametric, vector, position, velocity, speed, distance, slope of path, at rest.

Parametric Equations, Polar Coordinates,.skill: FUN-8.D Parametric Equations, Polar Coordinates, and Vector-Valued Functions - Analysis of Functions: Defining Polar Coordinates and Differentiating in Polar Form
Parametric Equations, Polar Coordinates,.prompt: This topic covers polar coordinates and differentiation for AP Calculus BC (CED: FUN-8.D, Unit 9). Point (r, theta): r = distance from origin, theta = angle from positive x-axis. Conversion: x = r cos theta, y = r sin theta. For r = f(theta), use parametric form x = f(theta)cos(theta), y = f(theta)sin(theta). dy/dx = (dy/dtheta)/(dx/dtheta) using Product Rule for dx/dtheta and dy/dtheta. Common curves: circles, cardioids, roses, limacons. Horizontal tangent: dy/dtheta = 0, dx/dtheta != 0. Vertical: dx/dtheta = 0, dy/dtheta != 0. AP exam note: polar differentiation requires careful Product Rule application. Students should identify tangent line slopes on polar curves. Vocabulary: polar coordinates, r, theta, conversion, dy/dx, Product Rule, cardioid, rose, limacon.

Parametric Equations, Polar Coordinates,.skill: CHA-5.D Parametric Equations, Polar Coordinates, and Vector-Valued Functions - Change: Finding the Area of a Polar Region or the Area Bounded by a Single Polar Curve
Parametric Equations, Polar Coordinates,.prompt: This topic covers polar area for AP Calculus BC (CED: CHA-5.D, Unit 9). A = (1/2) integral from alpha to beta of [f(theta)]^2 d(theta). This sums infinitesimal triangular sectors. Key: choose limits to trace the region exactly once. For curves passing through the origin, determine where r = 0. Common applications: area inside cardioids, rose petals, circles. Students must sketch curves and identify correct angular bounds. AP exam note: correct limits of integration are crucial. Students should know when curves complete full traversals. Vocabulary: polar area, sector, half r-squared, limits of integration, cardioid, rose petal, trace once.

Parametric Equations, Polar Coordinates,.skill: CHA-5.D Parametric Equations, Polar Coordinates, and Vector-Valued Functions - Change: Finding the Area of the Region Bounded by Two Polar Curves
Parametric Equations, Polar Coordinates,.prompt: This topic covers area between polar curves for AP Calculus BC (CED: CHA-5.D, Unit 9). A = (1/2) integral from alpha to beta of ([f(theta)]^2 - [g(theta)]^2)d(theta), where f is the outer curve and g is the inner. Find intersections by solving f(theta) = g(theta). Determine which curve is outer in each region. May need to split into separate integrals. Watch for origin intersections where both curves are zero at different theta values. AP exam note: polar area between curves requires careful identification of intersection angles and outer/inner curves. Sketch both curves on the same grid. Vocabulary: polar area, outer curve, inner curve, intersection, angular bounds, split integral.

Infinite Sequences and Series.skill: LIM-7.A Infinite Sequences and Series - Limits: Defining Convergent and Divergent Infinite Series
Infinite Sequences and Series.prompt: This topic covers infinite series fundamentals for AP Calculus BC (CED: LIM-7.A, Unit 10). An infinite series sum(a_n) is the limit of partial sums: S = lim S_N. Converges if S is finite; diverges otherwise. Distinguish between sequence {a_n} and series sum(a_n). Necessary condition: convergence requires lim a_n = 0 (but not sufficient -- harmonic series). BC students build on this to apply multiple convergence tests. Understanding partial sums is essential for error bounds and approximations. AP exam note: foundational understanding of series convergence is critical for the entire BC series unit. Vocabulary: infinite series, partial sum, convergent, divergent, sequence, limit, necessary condition.

Infinite Sequences and Series.skill: LIM-7.A Infinite Sequences and Series - Limits: Working with Geometric Series
Infinite Sequences and Series.prompt: This topic covers geometric series for AP Calculus BC (CED: LIM-7.A, Unit 10). Form: sum(a*r^n) = a/(1-r) when |r| < 1; diverges when |r| >= 1. Partial sums: S_N = a(1-r^N)/(1-r). Identify a (first term) and r (common ratio). Applications: repeating decimals, telescoping via partial fractions, power series as generalized geometric series (sum(x^n) = 1/(1-x) for |x| < 1). BC students connect geometric series to power series representations. AP exam note: geometric series are foundational. Students should recognize them in various forms and connect 1/(1-x) = sum(x^n) to Taylor series. Vocabulary: geometric series, common ratio, sum formula, convergence condition, power series connection.

Infinite Sequences and Series.skill: LIM-7.A Infinite Sequences and Series - Limits: The nth Term Test for Divergence
Infinite Sequences and Series.prompt: This topic covers the nth Term Test for AP Calculus BC (CED: LIM-7.A, Unit 10). If lim a_n != 0 or DNE, then sum(a_n) diverges. If lim a_n = 0, the test is inconclusive. Always apply this test first. The harmonic series shows that lim a_n = 0 is necessary but not sufficient for convergence. BC students should evaluate sequence limits using L'Hopital's Rule, comparison, and algebraic techniques before proceeding to other convergence tests. AP exam note: the nth Term Test is the first step in any convergence analysis. Students must clearly state 'inconclusive' when the limit is zero. Vocabulary: nth Term Test, Divergence Test, necessary condition, inconclusive, harmonic series, limit.

Infinite Sequences and Series.skill: LIM-7.A Infinite Sequences and Series - Limits: Integral Test for Convergence
Infinite Sequences and Series.prompt: This topic covers the Integral Test for AP Calculus BC (CED: LIM-7.A, Unit 10). If f is positive, continuous, and decreasing on [1,inf) with a_n = f(n), then sum(a_n) and integral of f(x)dx from 1 to infinity either both converge or both diverge. Conditions must be verified. The test does not give the series sum. Classic use: proving p-series convergence (p > 1) and divergence (p <= 1). BC students use the Integral Test when the integrand has a known antiderivative. AP exam note: verify the three conditions when applying. The Integral Test connects series to improper integrals (BC only). Vocabulary: Integral Test, positive, continuous, decreasing, improper integral, p-series.

Infinite Sequences and Series.skill: LIM-7.A Infinite Sequences and Series - Limits: Harmonic Series and p-Series
Infinite Sequences and Series.prompt: This topic covers harmonic and p-series for AP Calculus BC (CED: LIM-7.A, Unit 10). Harmonic series sum(1/n) diverges. p-series sum(1/n^p): converges if p > 1, diverges if p <= 1. These serve as benchmark series for comparison tests. Key examples: sum(1/n^2) converges, sum(1/sqrt(n)) diverges, sum(1/n^3) converges. BC students use p-series extensively as comparison standards and in endpoint convergence analysis for power series. AP exam note: p-series convergence is foundational knowledge used throughout the series unit. Quick identification of p and convergence/divergence is essential. Vocabulary: harmonic series, p-series, convergence threshold, benchmark, comparison, exponent.

Infinite Sequences and Series.skill: LIM-7.A Infinite Sequences and Series - Limits: Comparison Tests for Convergence
Infinite Sequences and Series.prompt: This topic covers comparison tests for AP Calculus BC (CED: LIM-7.A, Unit 10). Direct Comparison: 0 <= a_n <= b_n with sum(b_n) converging implies sum(a_n) converges; a_n >= b_n >= 0 with sum(b_n) diverging implies sum(a_n) diverges. Limit Comparison: if lim(a_n/b_n) = L with 0 < L < inf, both series share the same convergence behavior. Choose comparison series (p-series or geometric) based on dominant terms for large n. AP exam note: Limit Comparison is often easier than Direct Comparison. Students must show the comparison series converges/diverges. Vocabulary: Direct Comparison, Limit Comparison, positive terms, inequality, dominant term, benchmark series.

Infinite Sequences and Series.skill: LIM-7.A Infinite Sequences and Series - Limits: Alternating Series Test for Convergence
Infinite Sequences and Series.prompt: This topic covers the Alternating Series Test for AP Calculus BC (CED: LIM-7.A, Unit 10). For sum((-1)^n b_n) with b_n > 0: if b_n is decreasing and lim b_n = 0, the series converges. This convergence is conditional if sum(b_n) diverges. Classic: alternating harmonic sum((-1)^(n+1)/n) converges to ln(2). Partial sums oscillate around the sum. BC students connect this to the error bound (Topic 10.10) and absolute/conditional convergence analysis. AP exam note: both conditions must be verified explicitly. The AST is paired with the error bound on many BC exams. Vocabulary: Alternating Series Test, Leibniz Test, decreasing, limit zero, conditional convergence, oscillating.

Infinite Sequences and Series.skill: LIM-7.A Infinite Sequences and Series - Limits: Ratio Test for Convergence
Infinite Sequences and Series.prompt: This topic covers the Ratio Test for AP Calculus BC (CED: LIM-7.A, Unit 10). Compute L = lim|a_(n+1)/a_n|. L < 1: converges absolutely. L > 1 or infinity: diverges. L = 1: inconclusive. Best for series with factorials and exponentials. For power series sum(c_n(x-a)^n), the Ratio Test determines the radius of convergence R: |x-a| < R when L < 1. BC students use the Ratio Test as the primary tool for finding radius of convergence. AP exam note: the Ratio Test is one of the most important and frequently tested convergence tests on BC. Careful simplification of the ratio is essential. Vocabulary: Ratio Test, absolute convergence, factorial, exponential, radius of convergence, inconclusive.

Infinite Sequences and Series.skill: LIM-7.A Infinite Sequences and Series - Limits: Determining Absolute or Conditional Convergence
Infinite Sequences and Series.prompt: This topic covers absolute vs. conditional convergence for AP Calculus BC (CED: LIM-7.A, Unit 10). Absolute convergence: sum(|a_n|) converges. Conditional: sum(a_n) converges but sum(|a_n|) diverges. Absolute convergence implies convergence. Procedure: (1) test sum(|a_n|) with appropriate positive series test; (2) if converges, it is absolute; (3) if diverges, test sum(a_n) (often AST); (4) if sum(a_n) converges, it is conditional. This classification is important at power series endpoints. AP exam note: students must test both the original and absolute value series and clearly state the type of convergence. Vocabulary: absolute convergence, conditional convergence, series of absolute values, classification, endpoint.

Infinite Sequences and Series.skill: LIM-7.B Infinite Sequences and Series - Limits: Alternating Series Error Bound
Infinite Sequences and Series.prompt: This topic covers the alternating series error bound for AP Calculus BC (CED: LIM-7.B, Unit 10). For a convergent alternating series: |S - S_N| <= |a_(N+1)| = b_(N+1). The error is bounded by the first omitted term. To achieve accuracy epsilon, find smallest N with b_(N+1) < epsilon. This is a simple and powerful error bound specific to alternating series. BC students use it to determine how many terms are needed for a desired accuracy and to bound errors in Taylor polynomial approximations of alternating series. AP exam note: frequently tested. Students must identify the first omitted term correctly. Vocabulary: error bound, alternating series, first omitted term, accuracy, partial sum, truncation.

Infinite Sequences and Series.skill: LIM-8.A Infinite Sequences and Series - Limits: Finding Taylor Polynomial Approximations of Functions
Infinite Sequences and Series.prompt: This topic covers Taylor polynomials for AP Calculus BC (CED: LIM-8.A, Unit 10). The nth-degree Taylor polynomial centered at a: P_n(x) = sum[k=0 to n] f^(k)(a)(x-a)^k/k!. Maclaurin polynomial: a = 0. Higher degree generally gives better approximation near a. The polynomial matches f through its first n derivatives at a. Common Maclaurin polynomials: e^x, sin x, cos x, 1/(1-x), ln(1+x). BC students construct Taylor polynomials as part of series analysis and error bound problems. AP exam note: constructing Taylor polynomials is a core BC free-response skill. Students must compute derivatives accurately and apply the formula correctly. Vocabulary: Taylor polynomial, Maclaurin polynomial, center, degree, derivatives, approximation, coefficient.

Infinite Sequences and Series.skill: LIM-8.B Infinite Sequences and Series - Limits: Lagrange Error Bound
Infinite Sequences and Series.prompt: This topic covers the Lagrange Error Bound for AP Calculus BC (CED: LIM-8.B, Unit 10). |f(x) - P_n(x)| <= M|x-a|^(n+1)/(n+1)! where M bounds |f^(n+1)(c)| for c between a and x. Key: M must be an upper bound on the (n+1)th derivative on the entire interval. The bound decreases as n increases (usually) and as x gets closer to a. Works for any Taylor polynomial. BC students use this to determine how many terms are needed for a specified accuracy. AP exam note: Lagrange Error Bound is a challenging but commonly tested BC topic. Students must correctly identify M and set up the inequality. Vocabulary: Lagrange Error Bound, remainder, Taylor polynomial, upper bound, (n+1)th derivative, accuracy.

Infinite Sequences and Series.skill: LIM-8.C Infinite Sequences and Series - Limits: Radius and Interval of Convergence of Power Series
Infinite Sequences and Series.prompt: This topic covers radius and interval of convergence for AP Calculus BC (CED: LIM-8.C, Unit 10). Power series sum(c_n(x-a)^n) converges for |x-a| < R (radius) and diverges for |x-a| > R. Find R using the Ratio Test: R = 1/lim|c_(n+1)/c_n| (or Root Test). Test endpoints separately using other convergence tests (p-series, AST, etc.). The interval may be open, half-open, or closed. Inside the radius: absolute convergence. At endpoints: may converge conditionally, absolutely, or diverge. BC students must master this for representing functions as power series. AP exam note: finding the interval of convergence with complete endpoint analysis is a signature BC free-response problem. Vocabulary: power series, radius of convergence, interval of convergence, endpoint analysis, Ratio Test, absolute.

Infinite Sequences and Series.skill: LIM-8.D Infinite Sequences and Series - Limits: Finding Taylor or Maclaurin Series for a Function
Infinite Sequences and Series.prompt: This topic covers finding Taylor/Maclaurin series for AP Calculus BC (CED: LIM-8.D, Unit 10). Taylor series: sum(f^(n)(a)/n! * (x-a)^n). Key known series: e^x = sum(x^n/n!), sin x = sum((-1)^n x^(2n+1)/(2n+1)!), cos x = sum((-1)^n x^(2n)/(2n)!), 1/(1-x) = sum(x^n), ln(1+x) = sum((-1)^(n+1) x^n/n). Derive new series by: computing derivatives and finding patterns, substitution into known series (e.g., e^(-x^2)), differentiation/integration of known series, multiplication/addition. AP exam note: BC students must memorize common series and manipulate them fluently. Series manipulation is one of the most tested BC skills. Vocabulary: Taylor series, Maclaurin series, known series, substitution, manipulation, coefficient pattern.

Infinite Sequences and Series.skill: LIM-8.E Infinite Sequences and Series - Limits: Representing Functions as Power Series
Infinite Sequences and Series.prompt: This topic covers representing functions as power series for AP Calculus BC (CED: LIM-8.E, Unit 10). Techniques: (1) substitution into known series; (2) term-by-term differentiation; (3) term-by-term integration (determine C with initial conditions); (4) multiplication/addition of series. Example: integral of e^(-t^2)dt has no elementary form but its series is obtained by integrating the series for e^(-t^2) term by term. The interval of convergence may change at endpoints after differentiation or integration (radius stays the same). AP exam note: representing functions as power series is a classic BC free-response question. Students should justify convergence and use series operations fluently. Vocabulary: power series representation, term-by-term differentiation, term-by-term integration, substitution, interval.
